name: Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions
core_claim: Robert Plutchik's psychoevolutionary theory of emotion (1980, 'Emotion: A Psychoevolutionary Synthesis', and the iconic wheel/circumplex diagram) claims that emotion is a dimensional, structured system built from a small set of eight BASIC emotions that evolved as adaptive responses to fundamental survival situations. The eight primaries are arranged as four bipolar opposing pairs: joy vs sadness, trust vs disgust, fear vs anger, and anticipation vs surprise. Three further structural claims define the model: (1) INTENSITY — each basic emotion exists on a graded continuum (e.g. annoyance -> anger -> rage; serenity -> joy -> ecstasy), depicted as the vertical/radial dimension of the cone, so the wheel is really a three-dimensional cone collapsed to a circle; (2) SIMILARITY — emotions adjacent on the wheel are psychologically similar and those opposite are antithetical, defining a circumplex of relatedness; (3) COMBINATION — complex emotions ('dyads') are blends of adjacent or near primaries (e.g. joy + trust = love; trust + fear = submission; fear + surprise = awe; anger + anticipation = aggressiveness; joy + anticipation = optimism; disgust + sadness = remorse; sadness + surprise = disappointment; anger + disgust = contempt). Applied to STORY ANALYSIS, the claim becomes: the emotional content a narrative evokes (in characters and in viewers) can be decomposed into these eight primaries and their intensity-graded blends, giving a fixed, finite emotional palette for tagging what a story makes you feel. It is a taxonomy of DISCRETE-but-DIMENSIONAL affect, not of narrative structure or topic.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Squarely EXPERIENTIAL/AFFECTIVE, not descriptive-topical. The wheel is a taxonomy of FELT EMOTION — what an emotional episode IS and feels like — not of what a story is about (no topics, no plot, no morality) and not of narrative structure (no arcs, no turning points). As applied to story analysis it occupies the 'what does it make you feel' dimension: the discrete emotional textures a work evokes (fear, joy, disgust, anticipation, their blends). It is more granular and categorical than a single valence/arousal axis: rather than 'positive/negative' it names eight qualitatively distinct affects plus their intensities and blends. Note an ambiguity that matters for application: the wheel can describe (a) the emotions DEPICTED in characters, (b) the emotions a viewer is intended/likely to FEEL, or (c) the emotional 'tone' attributed to the work. These are different objects; the framework itself is a psychological model of an individual's emotion and does not specify which target applies in narrative use.
key_dimensions: Concrete axes the model proposes: (1) EIGHT BASIC EMOTIONS as nominal categories — joy, trust, fear, surprise, sadness, disgust, anger, anticipation. (2) FOUR BIPOLAR PAIRS (opposition axis): joy<->sadness, trust<->disgust, fear<->anger, anticipation<->surprise. (3) INTENSITY (radial/vertical continuum): each primary graded from mild to intense, e.g. serenity/joy/ecstasy; acceptance/trust/admiration; apprehension/fear/terror; distraction/surprise/amazement; pensiveness/sadness/grief; boredom/disgust/loathing; annoyance/anger/rage; interest/anticipation/vigilance. (4) SIMILARITY/CIRCUMPLEX ARRANGEMENT (angular distance = relatedness). (5) DYADS (combinations): primary dyads from adjacent emotions (love = joy+trust; submission = trust+fear; awe = fear+surprise; disapproval = surprise+sadness; remorse = sadness+disgust; contempt = disgust+anger; aggressiveness = anger+anticipation; optimism = anticipation+joy), plus secondary and tertiary dyads from more distant pairs. The candidate-vocabulary axes for HelpME2C are the eight primaries, the four bipolar tensions, the intensity grade, and the named dyads as compound emotional labels.
cross_cultural_validity: Mixed, and a recognised weak point. Plutchik's basis is psychoevolutionary — he argued the eight primaries are universal adaptive reactions shared across phylogeny, which is a strong universality claim aligned with (but broader than) Ekman's basic-emotion tradition. There IS partial cross-cultural support for some discrete basic emotions (notably recognition of facial expressions of joy, fear, anger, sadness, disgust, surprise across many cultures), but Plutchik's SPECIFIC structure — the exact eight, the particular opposing pairs, the dyad blends, and the inclusion of 'trust' and 'anticipation' as primaries — is much less validated than the general claim that some emotions are basic. Critics from cross-cultural psychology and anthropology challenge universal emotion taxonomies altogether (emotions are valued, expressed, conceptualised, and lexicalised differently across cultures; some languages lack one-to-one translations for 'disgust', 'trust', or 'anticipation'). Recent computational work flags concrete breakage: e.g. 'trust' in Arabic-narrative contexts spans reassurance, confidence, and relational sensibility that diverges from Plutchik's Western definition. So: the general 'some emotions are basic and recognisable' claim has moderate cross-cultural support; Plutchik's exact wheel structure is implicitly Western/English-centric and not robustly validated across languages. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Weak as a structural model; the framework is theoretically derived (psychoevolutionary synthesis) more than data-driven, and a recurring criticism is that the precise WHEEL STRUCTURE — the eight-fold set, the bipolar oppositions, and especially the dyad-combination rules — receives little direct empirical support. There is no strong experimental validation that complex emotions are literally blends of those specific primaries, nor that exactly these eight are the right primitives (the model is also criticised for omitting emotions like pride, shame, guilt, and curiosity-as-distinct). Where the wheel has empirical traction is INSTRUMENTAL/COMPUTATIONAL: it is heavily used as a labelling scheme in affective computing and sentiment/emotion analysis of text (and film scripts), where its value is as a convenient, finer-than-polarity category set, not as a proven theory — its popularity in NLP reflects usability, not validation. Crucially, it has NOT been validated against narrative ENJOYMENT, COMPLETION, or PREFERENCE data: nobody has shown that a film evoking 'awe' or 'optimism' is more enjoyed or more completable. To validate LLM-extracted Plutchik dimensions one would: (a) check inter-rater/LLM-vs-human agreement on assigning the eight emotions / dyads to synopses or scenes; (b) test whether those emotion tags predict real viewer ratings, completion, or 'felt' self-reports; and (c) test the combination claim — whether perceived complex emotions actually decompose into the predicted primary pairs. Evidence quality today is mostly anecdotal/computational-convenience, not experimental-confirmatory.
operationalisability: Reasonably operationalisable as a LABEL SET from synopsis, but with real reliability caveats, and the natural extraction unit varies. The eight primaries are intuitive and an LLM can plausibly tag the dominant emotions a synopsis evokes ('a grieving widow' -> sadness; 'a tense hunt for a killer' -> fear + anticipation; 'a heartwarming reunion' -> joy + trust = love). Best extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK for tone/dominant-emotion tagging, but emotion shifts within a work, so SCENE/ARC-level is where the model is most accurate and SEASON-level for serialised TV; a single whole-work emotion label flattens deliberately mixed tonal palettes. Failure modes: (1) synopses describe PLOT/topic, not felt emotion, so the LLM must infer affect from situation, conflating 'story is about war' with 'story makes you feel fear' (a war drama might evoke sadness, not fear); (2) the depicted-vs-evoked ambiguity — characters' emotions vs viewer's emotions get conflated; (3) the dyad/combination layer is unreliable — 'awe = fear+surprise' style decomposition is fragile and LLMs will hallucinate clean blends where the real affect is messy; (4) intensity grading from a short synopsis is largely guesswork (annoyance vs rage rarely recoverable from marketing copy); (5) irony, comedy, and tonal mixing defeat single-emotion tags; (6) culturally specific emotion concepts get force-fit into the eight English categories; (7) genre priors leak (assuming every horror film's primary is fear). So: dominant primary-emotion tags are moderately extractable; precise intensities and dyad decompositions are NOT reliably extractable from synopsis alone.
motivation_served: Maps most directly to MOOD REGULATION and to the affective side of HEDONIC vs EUDAIMONIC enjoyment, by naming the specific feeling a viewer is seeking. Unlike a coarse valence axis, the wheel lets a viewer's want be specified emotionally: 'something joyful/feel-good' (joy), 'something thrilling/tense' (fear+anticipation/awe), 'a good cry' (sadness/grief -> eudaimonic being-moved), 'something that makes us anxious in a fun way' (apprehension), 'comforting and warm' (joy+trust=love). It is therefore a precise mood-targeting vocabulary. For GROUP viewing it offers an unusually concrete negotiation handle: two people can each state a desired emotional destination ('I want to laugh', 'I want tension') and the recommender can find works whose emotional palette overlaps, or trade off — one person's appetite for fear/dread versus another's preference for joy/trust. Because the eight primaries are bipolar, conflicts are legible (one wants joy, the other is fine with sadness), and blends like 'optimism' or 'love' may serve as compromise targets both parties accept. It speaks to the 'how do we want to feel tonight' question more granularly than valence alone.
receiver_dependence: Genuinely MIXED, and the application target determines which. If the wheel tags emotions DEPICTED in the work (characters' fear, a tragic death) those are closer to WORK-SIDE/ABSOLUTE properties estimable from the text. If it tags the emotion the work EVOKES IN THE VIEWER, that is RELATIONAL — the same scene evokes terror in one viewer and boredom in another, depending on sensitivity, mood, prior exposure, and taste (a horror fan feels excitement where another feels distress). The intensity grade is especially receiver-dependent (one viewer's 'apprehension' is another's 'terror'). For a two-person recommender the clean move is to split: store a WORK-SIDE 'intended/dominant emotional palette' (which primaries the work foregrounds — relatively stable, synopsis/genre-estimable) as an absolute attribute, and model each viewer's EMOTION PREFERENCE/TOLERANCE separately (this user seeks fear, that user avoids it; this user loves tearjerkers). The joint problem then matches one work-side palette against two user-side affect-preference profiles. The framework itself is a model of an individual's felt emotion, so its native object is receiver-side; the application has to deliberately project it onto work-side attributes to be tractable.
blind_spots: (1) It is a model of EMOTION CATEGORIES only — it says nothing about plot, theme, morality, character, meaning, structure, or arc; two utterly different stories evoking 'fear' are indistinguishable to it. (2) The specific eight are contested and OMIT salient emotions — pride, shame, guilt, jealousy, nostalgia, curiosity, embarrassment, contempt-as-primary — many of which are highly relevant to drama; 'trust' and 'anticipation' as primaries are odd fits for felt narrative emotion. (3) The dyad-combination claim (love = joy+trust, awe = fear+surprise) is intuitively neat but empirically weak and easily over-applied. (4) It captures momentary/episodic emotion, not the TRAJECTORY of feeling over a work (Reagan's emotional arcs do that); a single emotion label cannot express 'starts joyful, ends devastating'. (5) It collapses the depicted/evoked/tonal distinction. (6) Intensity is poorly recoverable and viewer-relative. (7) It ignores AROUSAL/pacing as an independent axis, comedic rhythm, suspense mechanics, and aesthetic/cognitive pleasures (cleverness, beauty, intellectual engagement) that are not reducible to the eight affects. (8) Mixed/ambiguous/ironic tones — the lifeblood of sophisticated narrative — resist clean primary tagging. (9) Cross-cultural and re-watch/social dynamics are outside scope. (10) No link to enjoyment: knowing a film evokes disgust says nothing about whether anyone wants to watch it.
vocabulary_implications: Plutchik supplies a discrete EMOTIONAL-PALETTE facet, parallel and complementary to Reagan's emotional-trajectory facet (categories of feeling vs shape of feeling over time) and distinct from the topical/moral/identity theme facets. Recommended encoding: a small controlled 'evoked-emotion' facet the LLM tags from synopsis, restricted to the robustly-extractable primaries and a handful of high-value dyads, NOT the full intensity-and-dyad apparatus. Proposed slugs (work-side dominant-emotion palette, multi-select): 'evokes-fear-dread', 'evokes-joy-warmth', 'evokes-sadness-grief', 'evokes-tension-anticipation', 'evokes-disgust-revulsion', 'evokes-wonder-awe' (fear+surprise dyad), 'evokes-tenderness-love' (joy+trust dyad), 'evokes-anger-outrage'. Keep this facet small and tonal; treat it as 'how it leaves you feeling', complementary to genre. STRONG flags on extractability: (a) intensity grading (annoyance vs rage) is NOT reliably synopsis-extractable — do not store as certain; (b) the full dyad decomposition is fragile — only ship dyads that are themselves intuitive labels (love, awe), not as computed blends; (c) evoked-emotion tags should be marked lower-confidence than topical tags because they require affect inference from plot description; (d) the work-side palette is the storable object — the user's affinity for each emotion (seeks fear / avoids sadness) is a SEPARATE user-side preference, not a synopsis-extractable work attribute. Backstop with audience tags (e.g. 'scary', 'heartwarming', 'tearjerker') and reviews where available.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Plutchik operates at a different LAYER than most existing HelpME2C slugs — it tags FELT EMOTION whereas the current vocabulary is overwhelmingly topical/structural — so it largely ADDS a new facet rather than validating existing slugs. It cross-cuts and emotionally annotates many existing themes: 'grief-and-loss', 'meditation-on-death', 'war-and-its-toll' carry sadness/grief; 'existential-horror', 'cosmic-horror', 'folk-horror', 'survival-and-endurance', 'chase-and-escape' carry fear+anticipation (and the awe dyad for cosmic horror); 'found-family', 'platonic-soulmates', 'forbidden-love', 'coming-of-age' carry joy+trust (love); 'power-corrupts', 'betrayal-and-forgiveness', 'corruption-and-cover-up', 'vengeance-and-its-cost' carry anger/disgust/contempt; 'mystery-investigation', 'whodunit', 'heist-and-caper' run on anticipation+surprise. It clarifies that the existing vocabulary encodes WHAT a story is about but not HOW it feels, and the gap it reveals is exactly an evoked-emotion facet. It does NOT suggest any existing slug is misaligned (they are topical, not affective), and unlike Reagan it adds emotion KIND rather than emotion DIRECTION/shape — the two are complementary (palette x trajectory). It overlaps partly with genre signals (horror=fear) but is finer because it separates, e.g., a disgust-driven body-horror from a dread-driven slow-burn within the same genre.
key_sources: Plutchik, R. (1980). Emotion: A Psychoevolutionary Synthesis. Harper & Row (PAYWALLED / out of print; foundational text). Plutchik, R. (2001). 'The Nature of Emotions', American Scientist, 89(4):344-350 — the canonical accessible summary of the wheel (often available free as PDF; journal page: https://www.americanscientist.org/article/the-nature-of-emotions ). Plutchik, R. & Conte, H. R. (eds, 1997). Circumplex Models of Personality and Emotions, APA (PAYWALLED). Robert Plutchik — Wikipedia (free overview): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Plutchik . Emotion classification — Wikipedia (free, situates Plutchik vs Ekman/circumplex and notes criticisms incl. omission of pride/shame and lack of empirical support for wheel structure): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emotion_classification . Critique: Smith, H. & Schneider, A. (2009). 'Critiquing Models of Emotions', Sociological Methods & Research (PAYWALLED; ResearchGate listing: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/249693833 ). Computational-application surveys (free, arXiv): Kim & Klinger, 'A Survey on Sentiment and Emotion Analysis for Computational Literary Studies', arXiv:1808.03137, https://arxiv.org/pdf/1808.03137 ; 'Deciphering Emotions in Children Storybooks' (uses Plutchik, notes cross-cultural 'trust' divergence in Arabic), arXiv:2506.18201, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.18201 . Film-script application: 'Emotion and Sentiment Analysis from a Film Script: A Case Study', J. Digital Contents Society (KoreaScience, free): https://www.koreascience.or.kr/article/JAKO201707356128225.page .
